stem: Why is hybrid encryption (symmetric + asymmetric) so often used?

choices.text: Because symmetric encryption is incapable of encrypting data.
To automatically double the key size.
To combine the secure key exchange of asymmetric crypto with the speed of symmetric crypto on the data.
Because asymmetric crypto needs no key at all.

explanation: Asymmetric crypto (convenient but slow) is reserved for securely exchanging a key, then the data is encrypted with that fast symmetric key.

stem: What is the purpose of a salt when hashing a password?

choices.text: To make the hash reversible with a key.
To make every digest unique and defeat rainbow tables, even when two users have the same password.
To encrypt the password so it can be recovered later.
To speed up computing the digest.

explanation: A random, unique salt makes the digests of identical passwords differ and renders precomputed tables useless.

stem: What roles do asymmetric and symmetric encryption play in a TLS session?

choices.text: Only hashing is used; there is never any encryption.
Symmetric crypto authenticates the server, and asymmetric crypto encrypts every byte of data.
All the traffic is encrypted end to end with asymmetric crypto.
Asymmetric crypto authenticates the server and helps establish the session key, then symmetric crypto encrypts the data.

explanation: TLS relies on asymmetric crypto to authenticate and agree on a key, then encrypts the traffic with a fast symmetric algorithm such as AES-GCM.

stem: How is a digital signature produced and verified?

choices.text: You sign (the digest of the message) with the private key; you verify with the public key.
You sign with the public key; you verify with the private key.
You sign and verify with the same shared secret key.
You encrypt the entire message with the recipient’s public key.

explanation: Signing is done with the signer’s private key (over the digest of the message) and verified with their public key.

stem: Why should passwords not be stored with plain SHA-256?

choices.text: Because SHA-256 is reversible and therefore far too dangerous.
Because SHA-256 produces collisions systematically.
Because SHA-256 is too fast: an attacker tests billions of candidates per second; you need a slow, tunable KDF (Argon2, bcrypt, scrypt).
Because SHA-256 is incapable of processing text.

explanation: SHA-256 is built for speed, which makes brute-force cracking easy; passwords require a deliberately slow and memory-hungry function.

stem: Which statement about block cipher modes is correct?

choices.text: ECB is the most secure mode because each block is encrypted independently.
ECB leaks patterns (identical blocks give identical ciphertext), whereas GCM provides authenticated encryption.
CBC on its own guarantees the integrity and authenticity of the data.
Reusing a nonce in GCM with the same key has no consequences.

explanation: ECB reproduces the patterns of the plaintext and offers no serious security, whereas GCM (AEAD) adds integrity and authenticity to the encryption.

stem: What does a padding oracle attack (CBC) rely on?

choices.text: On knowing the encryption key beforehand.
On reusing a nonce in GCM.
On a collision in the hash function.
On a signal (an error or a timing difference) revealing whether the decrypted padding is valid, which allows the plaintext to be recovered byte by byte without the key.

explanation: The attacker manipulates the previous block and exploits the “padding valid / invalid” information returned by the oracle to reconstruct the plaintext, without ever holding the key.

stem: What is a timing side-channel attack?

choices.text: An attack that mathematically breaks the AES algorithm.
An attack that guesses the key by exhaustive brute force.
An attack that deduces a secret from the execution time (e.g. a comparison that stops at the first differing byte).
An attack that exploits a collision in the hash function.

explanation: Timing attacks target the implementation: variations in duration give the secret away, hence the use of constant-time comparisons.
